topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The small, unassuming package sat on my kitchen counter, a vibrant explosion of crimson roses and pale yellow lilies. There was no note. Just the flowers, wrapped in simple, crinkled brown paper. I picked one up, the velvety petals cool against my fingertips, and held it under the light. My breath hitched, a sudden, sharp intake of air that seemed to steal the oxygen from the room. Who would do this? I stared at the arrangement, my mind cycling through every possible explanation—a prank, a mistake, a bizarre public gesture—but nothing fit the sheer, unexpected beauty of it. My fingers trembled slightly as I tried to place the stems in a vase, the silence of the apartment amplifying the frantic thumping in my chest.

[story 2]

When the delivery person handed me the bouquet, the bright colours seemed to vibrate. I took the box, my hand feeling clumsy and oversized as I cradled the arrangement. The sheer lack of accompanying text left a gaping void in my expectation. I looked up, expecting a rushed apology or a hastily scribbled message, but there was only a blank space where a card should have been. My jaw went slack. I blinked several times, trying to process the visual information, but the reality felt too rich, too significant for such an unannounced presentation. I stood frozen by the front door, the cold air suddenly sharp against my skin, unable to move toward the entryway.

[story 3]

It was just sitting there, on the welcome mat, a perfect, silent offering. I approached it cautiously, my steps slow, almost hesitant, as if approaching a wild animal. The scent of the blooms—a heady mix of sweet perfume and damp earth—hit me full force. I reached out, my hand hovering above the arrangement for a long moment, my eyes wide and unblinking. My internal monologue stalled; my brain simply refused to generate a coherent response to the spectacle before me. I let out a small, involuntary sound, a soft gasp, and then I slowly lowered my hand, my gaze fixed on the petals, completely unable to formulate a single word.
